coredata.dc:title: Formation of dendritic structure in the diffusion zone of the bonded Zircaloy-4 and stainless steel 316L in the presence of Ti interlayer
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Akhter, J.I.
Ahmad, M.
Iqbal, M.
Akhtar, M.
Shaikh, M.A.
coredata.dc:description: Microstructure of the diffusion bonded samples of stainless steel 316L and Zircaloy-4 in the presence of Ti interlayer at 1000°C and 1050°C is investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and the phases in the diffusion zone (DZ) have been analyzed by energy dispersive system (EDS) attached with SEM. Five distinct regions have been observed in the sample bonded at 1000°C due to the diffusion of various elements from both stainless steel and Zircaloy-4. Local melting and formation of dendritic structure occurred in the sample bonded at 1050°C. Dendritic structure is achieved, which hinders cracking during solidification in the DZ.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dendritic structure
Diffusion bonding
Liquidus structure
Solidification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM micrograph showing the view of diffusion bonded sample of Zircaloy-4 and SS-316L in the presence of Ti interlayer at 1000°C.
SEM micrograph showing different regions in the DZ of diffusion bonded sample of Zircaloy-4 and SS-316L in the presence of Ti interlayer at 1000°C.
SEM micrograph showing the formation of different regions in the diffusion bonded sample of Zircaloy-4 and SS-316L in the presence of Ti interlayer at 1050°C.
SEM micrograph showing the dendritic structure in the DZ region II of diffusion bonded sample of Zircaloy-4 and SS-316L in the presence of Ti interlayer at 1050°C.

SEM micrograph at high magnification showing liquidus structure in the DZ and Cr rich layer near SS-316L in the diffusion bonded sample of Zircaloy-4 and SS-316L in the presence of Ti interlayer at 1050°C.
Quantitative analysis of different regions formed in the DZ of Zircaloy-4 and SS-316L in the presence of Ti interlayer at 1000°C
Quantitative analysis of the different regions formed in the DZ of Zircaloy-4 and SS-316L in the presence of Ti interlayer at 1050°C

article.ce:title: Formation of dendritic structure in the diffusion zone of the bonded Zircaloy-4 and stainless steel 316L in the presence of Ti interlayer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Research Division, Pakistan Institute of Nuclear Science and Technology, P.O. Nilore, Islamabad, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microstructure of the diffusion bonded samples of stainless steel 316L and Zircaloy-4 in the presence of Ti interlayer at 1000°C and 1050°C is investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and the phases in the diffusion zone (DZ) have been analyzed by energy dispersive system (EDS) attached with SEM. Five distinct regions have been observed in the sample bonded at 1000°C due to the diffusion of various elements from both stainless steel and Zircaloy-4. Local melting and formation of dendritic structure occurred in the sample bonded at 1050°C. Dendritic structure is achieved, which hinders cracking during solidification in the DZ.

ce:section.ce:para: The joining of materials by conventional welding techniques becomes difficult if the physical properties such as melting temperatures and thermal expansion coefficients of the two materials differ a lot, as it is necessary to have controlled melting on both sides of the weld joints simultaneously. Even if this criterion is met, it may not be possible to have an appropriate joint when the two materials are metallurgically incompatible. This is because metallurgical incompatibility may lead to a weld zone and heat affected zone (HAZ) microstructure without adequate mechanical strength. Zircaloy-4 and stainless steel are important materials in the reactor technology as the first is used as a cladding material for the fuel elements and the second is used as structural material.

Zircaloy-4 bonded with stainless steel (304, 304L, 316 and 316L) is being used extensively in various technological applications concerning especially the pressure tubes and fuel elements in some nuclear power plants [1–3]. Joining of these two materials by fusion welding is undesirable because brittle intermetallic compounds such as ZrFe2, ZrFe3 and Zr(Cr,Fe)2 are formed in the welded zone [4] which reduce the mechanical strength as well as the corrosion resistance at the weld joints [5]. Furthermore, difference in the thermal expansion coefficients, allotropic phase transformations, and elastic moduli of Zr and steel cause high residual stresses during the cooling of welds. Welding of dissimilar materials by electron beam and laser beam can produce weld microstructure much different from that of conventional welding techniques. The success of these techniques lies mainly in the local melting and fast cooling that takes place during the course of the welding. Welding of Zircaloy-4 and SS-304 by electron beam was shown to produce weld joint with reduced HAZ as well as small area of DZ with very low density of harmful phases [6]. However, thickness of the samples to be welded is limited because of the requirement of high energy electron beam or high power laser beam for more penetration depth.

Many problems of welded joints between steel and Zircaloy can be overcome by the process of diffusion bonding [7–9], whereby even the local melting of the two alloys can be avoided. During diffusion bonding solid-state bond is created by interdiffusion of various elements across the interface. However, occurrence of large DZ comprising of deleterious intermetallic phases has been observed. Shaaban et al. [10] have studied diffusion bonding between Zircaloy-4 and SS-304 and reported formation of brittle intermetallic phases in the DZ. Many researchers have introduced diffusion barriers in the form of interlayer to minimize or to prevent the formation of intermetallic compounds in the DZ. Shaaban and Hammad [11] have used Fe, and Wayman et al. [12] have used Pt interlayer for this purpose and obtained improved properties of the bond. Diffusion bonding of SS-304L and Zircaloy-4 in the presence of Ta intermediate layer has been reported by Ahmad et al. [13] and different phases have been identified. Kale et al. [14] have tried Ti and Fe both as diffusion barriers in the bonding of Zircaloy-2 with SS-316 to restrict the formation of intermetallic phases in DZ. The aim of the present study is to investigate the bond of the Zircaloy-4 with SS-316L in the presence of Ti interlayer only to avoid the harmful phases and achieve the eutectic composition in the DZ with dendritic structure.

The nominal composition of the two alloys is given in Table 1 . Samples of the size 2cm×1cm×0.5cm were cut from SS-316L and Zircaloy-4 rods and were polished on a lapping machine using diamond paste up to 0.25μm. Samples were wrapped by Kanthal wire and a pressure was applied by putting them in a vice machine for 24h to introduce plastic deformation on both the surfaces. Diffusion bonding was carried out under vacuum of 2×10−7 bar at 1000°C and 1050°C for 4h. Samples were etched chemically in solution of H2O2:HNO3:HF with a volume ratio of 50:47:3. The microstructure was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and microanalysis was done by energy dispersive system (EDS) attached with SEM.

SEM examination of the samples bonded at 1000°C revealed narrow and smooth DZ between the two alloys as shown in Fig. 1 . The image at higher magnification reveals five distinct regions, marked as I to V in Fig. 2 . The quantitative composition of all the five regions is given in Table 2 . Analysis for each region is taken at 10 different points and the average of the minimum 10 readings is taken in each case. It is clear from the elemental composition that the regions I and II are rich in Zr and formed in the Zircaloy-4. The microstructure of the region I consists of α-Zr plates and β-Zr(Ti) and the region II mainly contains β-Zr(Ti). The region III consists of α-Ti(Zr), whereas the region IV consists of β-Ti(Zr, Fe, Ni). The concentration of Ti increases and that of Zr decreases towards the region IV. The region V is rich in Ti and Fe, and gives eutectic composition with liquidus type microstructure.

The DZ consists of mainly Ti and Zr as indicated by the composition of different regions. Both the elements form solid solutions α-Ti(Zr), β-Ti(Zr), α-Zr(Ti) and β-Zr(Ti) as Ti has complete miscibility with Zr and there exists a continuous series of solid solutions [15]. The concentration of Fe in the DZ is very small except in the region V near SS-316L. The formation of the eutectic in the region V is explained on the basis of the phase diagram of Fe–Ti [15]. It is clear from the composition and the microstructure of the DZ that Ti interlayer acts as a barrier against the diffusion of Fe, Cr, Mo and Ni towards Zircaloy-4. The width of the DZ having higher concentration of Zr compared with that of Fe is about four times larger and the reason being the difference in the activation energies required for the reaction, which are 90.07 and 35.4kcal/mol for Fe–Ti and Zr–Ti [16], respectively. Elements like Cr, Mo and Ni were not detected in the regions I–III because of higher activation energies of diffusion for these elements in Ti. Very small content of Cr and Ni is detected in the region IV while the Ni concentration is higher in region V. This indicates that activation energy required for the reaction between Cr–Ti, Ni–Ti and Mo–Ti is also high even when compared with Fe–Ti. The other reason for the absence of Cr and Ni in the regions I–III and very low concentration in the other two regions near the SS-316L may be low content of Cr and Ni as compared to Fe in SS-316L.

The diffusion bond between Zircaloy-4 and SS-316L, produced at 1050°C, in the presence of Ti interlayer was also examined in the SEM and a low magnification view is shown in Fig. 3 . It is clear that the DZ consists of three regions named as I, II and III. The composition of the three regions along with the phases determined is given in Table 3 . The region I, formed in Zircaloy-4 within the DZ, is rich in Zr and Ti in the form of β-Zr(Ti) and also contains α-Zr plates. The region II within Ti interlayer has fine dendritic microstructure along with eutectic composition as shown in Fig. 4 . The dendritic structure is rich in Ti, Zr, Fe, Cr and Ni while the eutectic also contains these elements but have higher content of Cr and low content of Ni. The liquidus is rich in Cr because the dendrite forms interface before the solidification front and rejects Cr in the liquid. The dendritic structure formed in the DZ has been reported to be resistant against the solidification cracking [17]. The region III formed in the SS-316L as a layer contains very small amount of Ti and Ni and is rich in Cr as shown clearly in Fig. 5 . Such Cr-rich layer has also been observed by the many researchers [6,13,18,19] in the diffusion-bonded stainless steel with Zircaloy-4. This layer acts as a barrier against the diffusion of Ti and Zr towards the SS-316L. The intermetallic phases like Zr(Cr,Fe)2, ZrFe2 and ZrFe3 were not observed in any of the heat treated couples due to the high activation energy needed for the reactivity of Cr and Fe with Ti. Therefore, Ti layer acts as a barrier in between the Zircaloy-4 and SS-316L, which hinders the nucleation and growth of the above-mentioned phases. According to the Fe–Ti binary phase diagram [15], FeTi, FeTi2 and Fe2Ti could form in the DZ. However, these phases are not detected with EDS in the present study. The basic criterion for the formation of any intermetallic phase in the DZ is that the phase must first nucleate and then grow. The intermetallic phase will nucleate only when two types of atoms attain the composition to the stoichiometry of that phase. This nucleated embryo will grow into a sizeable intermetallic phase only if the rates of the two diffusion species are such that the flux of species arriving at the interface is more than that leaving the phase. The estimated flux of Fe atoms entering the TiFe2 and TiFe phases is less than those leaving these phases by two orders of magnitude [14]. Therefore, the chances of formation of stoichiometric phases like FeTi, FeTi2, Fe2Ti, TiCr2, TiNi, Ti2Ni and TiNi3 are very rare in spite of the presence of Fe in regions II–V and that of Cr and Ni in the regions IV and V.

In conclusion, smooth and narrow bond is achieved without any cracking. The Ti interlayer is found to be beneficial as it acted as a barrier against the diffusion of Fe, Cr, Mo and Ni towards the Zircaloy-4. This barrier restricted the formation of intermetallic phases like Zr(Cr,Fe)2, Zr2Fe and Zr2Ni. Dendritic structure with eutectic matrix is achieved which hinders the hot cracking in DZ.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Pakistan Science Foundation for financial support under Project No. C-PINSTECH/ENGG (39). Thanks are also due to the staff of the Radiation Damage Group for assistance in the experimental work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Solid Phase Welding of Metals
Diffusion Bonding as a Production Process Information Package Series
